notes: Table 1 comparison row 'Current annual inflation rate', Iran column. 1987 data except demographics (midyear 1988). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 1 "How Do Iran-Iraq Economies Compare?" (1987 data; demographics midyear 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

variant_label: Prices — Current annual inflation rate -- Iraq
notes: Table 1 comparison row 'Current annual inflation rate', Iraq column. 1987 data except demographics (midyear 1988). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 1 "How Do Iran-Iraq Economies Compare?" (1987 data; demographics midyear 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

notes: Table 1 comparison row 'Unemployment rate', Iran column. 1987 data except demographics (midyear 1988). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 1 "How Do Iran-Iraq Economies Compare?" (1987 data; demographics midyear 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

variant_label: Labor & Employment — Unemployment rate -- Iraq
notes: Table 1 comparison row 'Unemployment rate', Iraq column. 1987 data except demographics (midyear 1988). [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 1 "How Do Iran-Iraq Economies Compare?" (1987 data; demographics midyear 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

variant_label: Government finance — Martyr's wife lump-sum benefit equivalent
notes: A martyr's wife could obtain the equivalent of a $30,000 lump-sum payment -- roughly two years' salary for an Iranian engineer -- plus $575/month apparently for life. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.4]

variant_label: Government finance — Martyr's family monthly stipend
notes: Plus $575 a month, apparently for life, alongside the lump sum; additional durable-goods benefits if the martyr left a large family. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.4]

variant_label: Infrastructure (Transport) — Iraq new highway construction
notes: About 2,100 kilometers of new highways were completed in Iraq between 1980 and 1986. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.8]

variant_label: Infrastructure (Transport) — New Iraq railway: Baghdad-Al Qa'im-Akashat line
notes: Since the start of the war, Iraq added a 520-kilometer railway line between Baghdad, Al Qa'im, and Akashat. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.8]

variant_label: Infrastructure (Transport) — New Iraq railway: Al Hadithah-Karkuk section
notes: Iraq also added a 250-kilometer railway section between Al Hadithah and Karkuk. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.8]

variant_label: Infrastructure & Technology — Iraq telephone line count growth
notes: Iraq more than doubled its number of phone lines to 1 million between 1980 and 1986. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

variant_label: Energy — Iran electricity generation capacity needed for adequate supply
notes: A country of Iran's size should have an electricity generation capacity of 25,000 megawatts to adequately supply private and industrial consumers. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

unit: megawatts (less than one-third of the 25,000 MW benchmark)
variant_label: Energy — Iran actual electricity generating capacity
notes: Iran's actual generating capacity is less than one-third of the 25,000 MW benchmark -- exact figure not printed in source, this is the implied upper bound of 'less than one-third'; treat as an upper-bound estimate, not a precise reported figure. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.9]

variant_label: Military expenditure — Financial requirements -- military imports
notes: Table 2 line item 'Financial requirements -- military imports' -- projected Iraq financing sources/requirements for 1988. Financing gap of -$5.7bn is sources minus requirements. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Table 2 "Iraq: Financing Sources and Requirements, 1988" (projected), November 1988, PDF p.12]

variant_label: Military expenditure — Western share of Iraq military deliveries, 1983 peak
notes: The West's share of military deliveries to Iraq (mostly by France) peaked at 35 percent in 1983 but has declined since. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.13]

variant_label: Military expenditure — Iran military imports source share -- Western Europe
notes: Figure 10 pie chart, Iran side: Western Europe supplied 30% of Iran's 1985-87 military imports. Note: printed shares (30+24+46=100) sum correctly. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 10 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Military Imports, 1985-87" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Military expenditure — Iran military imports source share -- China
notes: Figure 10 pie chart, Iran side: China supplied 24% of Iran's 1985-87 military imports. Note: printed shares (30+24+46=100) sum correctly. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 10 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Military Imports, 1985-87" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Military expenditure — Iran military imports source share -- Other
notes: Figure 10 pie chart, Iran side: Other supplied 46% of Iran's 1985-87 military imports. Note: printed shares (30+24+46=100) sum correctly. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 10 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Military Imports, 1985-87" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Military expenditure — Iraq military imports source share -- USSR
notes: Figure 10 pie chart, Iraq side: USSR supplied 63% of Iraq's 1985-87 military imports. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 10 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Military Imports, 1985-87" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Military expenditure — Iraq military imports source share -- China
notes: Figure 10 pie chart, Iraq side: China supplied 9% of Iraq's 1985-87 military imports. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 10 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Military Imports, 1985-87" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Military expenditure — Iraq military imports source share -- Western Europe
notes: Figure 10 pie chart, Iraq side: Western Europe supplied 11% of Iraq's 1985-87 military imports. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 10 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Military Imports, 1985-87" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Military expenditure — Iraq military imports source share -- Others
notes: Figure 10 pie chart, Iraq side: Others supplied 17% of Iraq's 1985-87 military imports. [cite: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 10 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Military Imports, 1985-87" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21]

variant_label: Military expenditure — Soviet Bloc share of Iraq arms purchases since 1979
notes: Bloc countries have supplied 60 percent of Baghdad's arms purchases since 1979, and delivered about 80 percent in 1987. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16]

variant_label: Military expenditure — Soviet Bloc share of Iraq arms deliveries, 1987
notes: The Bloc delivered about 80 percent of Iraq's arms in 1987. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16]

variant_label: Military expenditure — USSR share of Iraq arms deliveries, 1987
notes: The USSR alone, which delivered 63 percent of Iraq's arms last year (1987), supplied most of the Bloc share. [cite: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16]
